body.u: I now sit in my old chair staring at the walls, but really I am staring into my past. I can remmeberremember the very first day I started work. I had to travel on my moped about thirty miles to get there, very early in the morning. When I arrived I was shown into a large office where all the deskdesks were layedlaid out in rows with piles of paper on. I was shown what I had to do and left to get on with it.

Two years later I changed my job but remained in the same firm. I now began to enjoy my job, I became able to work at my own speed and I had people to which I give orders. I stayed working with computorscomputers until I was twenty-two, by this I was married and starting a family so I had no time for work.

As soon as my family started going to school I was left to myself all day in the house so I began to think about getting a job. But I didn’t want a computing job like my old one so I decided on a compleatlycompletely new carreercareer. I began training to become a teacher, but not of ordinary children, because I had always wanted to teach blind children. It toketook three years to complete the teaching course, but it was worth it. I got a job teaching blind children who were about six or seven. I got a lot of pleasure out of this job and although I did not start teaching untilluntil I was thirty-three I found the job quiet easy and very rewarding.

I became more aware of my society then and began to give up a few evenings a week to help out at the children’s homes and play groupesgroups. The more I help these children the more I felt I done something worthwhile with my life.

My family grew-up very quickly and were soon making plans for their own lifeslives. When they had left home, the house seamed empty, so my husband and I decided to adopt a child. It was not easy at first because of our ages. I was not forty and my husband was two years older. But because we had brought up a family of our own and I worked with children, we were accepted as a suitable couple to adopt children. We didntdidn't want a small white badybaby because I throughtthoughttotoo many badiesbabies were adopted, when we went to the adoption home, I saw a boy of seven who was all by himself, he was the one I choose. Some people said I was a fool to adopt him because he was black. But that never mattered to me.

I think he was given a good life, we treated as a son and grew to love him as much as if he was our own. I still carried on with my work at the childrenschildren's home and became more involved in helpindhelping.

I was fifty when my husband died and that meant my whole life changed. I had to sell my house and move into a small flat, along with the children I had adopted. I did not earn a lot of money teaching, but I did get a small widows allowance which helped out. As the boy was growing up and was not what is concideredconsidered a young man. I was able to leave him in the flat while I went out to work.

He left school at the age of sixteen so he could work and bring home money, so that I would not have needed to work so hard, instead of going onto collage. When he married he still sent me money each week to help pay the bills.

At fifty-six I remarried, more for companionship, than anything else. My new husband made me give up work as he earned enough to support us both. I was then able to devote all my time to helping children. At sixty I retired from helping at the blind school but continued to help at the childrenschildren's home. When my husband died I was sixty-five, so now I live alone, but I still visit the childrenschildren's home twice a week.
